content.assessment: This proposal contracts the selective track's footprint via thresholds and capacity caps but is explicitly not an admission-mechanism or curricular reform. The whole-system lens raises the central causal-attribution question: shrinking supply without addressing the selection process risks concentrating remaining scarce places among already-advantaged families, since it does not touch the mechanisms (coaching return, family capital, primary-school segregation) that determine who fills the remaining seats. The comparative evidence base for structural reform (Finland, France, Poland) indicates that quantity-only levers, unbundled from curricular and equity-targeted admission design, have not been the primary drivers of documented equity improvements elsewhere.
opportunities: Could be paired with admissions reform (as in TP-live-t1) to jointly shrink and de-bias the selective track
Demographic cover may reduce political resistance windows compared to explicit equity-framed reform
strengths: Uses existing ministerial discretionary levers already exercised on segregation grounds, suggesting some institutional precedent and feasibility
Aligns policy timing with demographic decline, potentially reducing the political salience of contraction as a lever distinct from ideological reversal
threats: Church and foundation schools outside relevant jurisdiction could absorb displaced demand, undermining net system-wide contraction and preserving a segregated channel
Director lobbying access is a documented political-economy risk that could reverse or dilute caps over time
weaknesses: Contraction of place numbers is not evidenced as sufficient on its own to improve equity; the comparative record emphasizes bundled curricular and admissions reform as necessary, not capacity management alone
Leaving admission mechanisms unchanged risks concentrating remaining places even more among advantaged families, worsening rather than improving equity ratios
